rule:
Definitions - For the purposes of this market:
- "Publicly launch" means the service is publicly advertised and available through the Tesla app or any publicly accessible booking mechanism.
- "Member of the general public" means any person who can summon a ride using the publicly available booking system.
- "Fully driverless" means the vehicle operates without an on‑board safety driver and without a remote tele‑operator actively steering, braking, or accelerating during normal operation; a remote tele‑operator may intervene only in a genuine emergency, which does not constitute regular control.
- "Genuine emergency" means an immediate safety‑critical situation in which human intervention is required to avoid collision, injury, or other imminent harm.
- "Consensus of credible reporting" means at least two independent credible sources report that the service meets the above criteria, and no credible source reports that it does not.

Interpretation hierarchy - If two regular rules conflict, the rule with the higher number (more specific) prevails. Meta‑rules take precedence over regular rules; if two meta‑rules conflict, the later‑numbered meta‑rule prevails.
The market resolves to "Yes" if Tesla publicly launches a fully driverless taxi service by 30 June 2024, 11:59 PM Eastern Time; otherwise, it resolves to "No".
Any service that satisfies all definitions in Meta‑Rule 1 (i.e., allows a member of the general public to summon and ride in a fully driverless Tesla vehicle) counts toward resolution.

A human may be present in the vehicle only as a safety observer not occupying the driver's seat. Remote tele‑operators may intervene only in a genuine emergency, as defined in Meta‑Rule 1, and such intervention does not constitute regular control.

Any program that is restricted to Tesla employees, invite‑only testers, closed‑beta participants, factory self‑delivery features, or the mere release of Full Self‑Driving software for private owner‑drivers does not qualify as a public launch.

Events that do not involve live ridership by members of the general public – such as regulatory permits or approvals, press demonstrations, prototype unveilings, or any other event not involving public ride‑booking – will not count toward resolution.
The market's resolution source will be a consensus of credible reporting as defined in Meta‑Rule 1.
